comments.author: @ricardodias736
comments.text:
14:21 🇵🇹 The first to use boats to transport slaves, transatlantic trade, there is nothing to be proud of, despite it being legal practice at the time, unfortunately. 

Our pride lies in being the first to abolish the slave trade. 
 Acknowledging and resolving the mistake is part of the process.

replies.author: @reallifeincountries
replies.text: A heavy but profoundly important piece of history to acknowledge. ⚓ 📜 History is rarely simple—it carries both deep scars and progress. How is this complex balance between the colonial past and the abolitionist pride taught and viewed in Portuguese schools today?"

replies.author: @mikidias
replies.text:
@DiscoverGlobePlacesTop10_NL158

Because we hate wars.
Local people and local men are too coward to mingle in wars.
Also the mindset. Locals are extremely jealous people 😞 🤢  
Here in my hometown nobody helps out each other if you're poor and helpless and defenseless. We're becoming the dirty US capitalist neo - Liberal Zionist of Europe 🇪🇺.

I've already myself travelled throughout all of Europe in the past and didn't liked it. Then, I travelled to middle - East Khallijiya 🇦🇪 and finally found - out "my place" 😇. Culture and people who help each other and are not cowards and stand - up for what's right and fights what's wrong. 👍🏻 
Now, my dream is to move - out from here back to middle - East Khallijiya and to settle down there.... 🧳 ✈️  
This Vídeo is just misleading for the poor middle class labour worker.... It's only good to live here if you have money and are rich. If you're poor like me and like 8️⃣0️⃣ % of the local folks, then you're literally screwed - up❗ ❕ 

This video is totally Bullshit and nonsense. It's not realistic and is misleading. Misinformation. 👎🏻 

Welcome to Portugal! 🇵🇹

replies.author: @reallifeincountries
replies.text: That is one of the most fascinating paradoxes in history. 🇵🇹 📜 From global empire to a sanctuary of peace. Do you think this transition is a sign of a nation that has truly found peace with its past, or did it just change its priorities?

comments.author: @RealLifeFilesDoc
comments.text: The tradition of Fado de Coimbra [26:50] is absolutely fascinating! Seeing those male students in long black capes singing love songs at midnight [27:11] feels like stepping into a time machine. And the fact that the audience shows respect by clearing their throats instead of applauding [27:30]? That is such a beautifully unique detail. Thank you for showcasing the "Portugal Profundo" that most tourists never see. Olá! 🇵🇹

replies.author: @TravelToFacts
replies.text: Which Portuguese tradition would you most like to experience?

replies.author: @reallifeincountries
replies.text: Deep respect for noticing that specific detail. The throat-clearing tradition is as raw as it gets. Do you think modern audiences should adopt unique respect habits like that, or is standard clapping better?
